$schema_note: Stage 1 Issues Study question bank. Each question is a complete philosophical question (NOT a topic). Used by the static site question-bank UI.

key_areas.label: Ethics
key_areas.tagline: Questions about how we should live, what we owe each other, and what makes an action right.

key_areas.label: Epistemology
key_areas.tagline: Questions about knowledge — what it is, how we get it, when we can trust it.

key_areas.label: Metaphysics
key_areas.tagline: Questions about what is real — minds, time, freedom, identity, the structure of the world.

virtue.label: Virtue, attention, and the good life

emotion_knowledge.label: Emotion as a way of knowing

science_knowing.label: Science and the limits of knowing

aesthetic_ontology.label: What is a work of art?

questions.question: Is a fetus a person, and does it matter morally if it is?
positions: Marquis: killing wrongs the being of a future like ours (FLO theory)
Thomson: even granting personhood, no right to use another's body
counter_positions: Singer: personhood depends on capacities, not species membership

questions.question: Does abortion involve the killing of a person, or the refusal to lend one's body to another?
positions: Killing-frame (Marquis, Catholic tradition)
Bodily-autonomy frame (Thomson)
counter_positions: Combined view: both questions matter and must be settled separately

questions.question: What features make something a person — biological humanity, sentience, rationality, or something else?
positions: Species membership (Catholic, Marquis-adjacent)
Sentience/capacity (Singer)
Rational agency (Kantian tradition)
counter_positions: Personhood as a relational rather than intrinsic property

questions.question: Are non-human animals moral persons?
positions: Yes for many animals (Singer)
Only for those with rational agency (Kantian view)
No animals are persons (traditional view)
counter_positions: Degrees of personhood proportional to cognitive capacity

questions.question: Could an artificial intelligence ever be a person, and what would it take?
positions: Yes if it has the right functional organisation (functionalist)
No because consciousness requires biology (biological naturalism)
The question is undecidable from the outside
counter_positions: The question itself misframes consciousness

questions.question: If personhood comes in degrees, can some people be 'more of a person' than others — and what follows for ethics?
positions: Degrees of moral consideration track degrees of personhood (Singer-extension)
All persons equal in dignity regardless of capacities (Kantian)
counter_positions: Degrees view collapses into discrimination

questions.question: Does brain death mean the person has died, even if the body is alive?
positions: Brain-death = person-death (functionalist)
Death only when the organism stops (biological view)
counter_positions: Different criteria for different purposes (organ donation vs grief)

questions.question: Is severe dementia a kind of dying — and does it change how we should treat the person?
positions: Personal identity has been disrupted (Lockean)
The same person remains throughout (continuity view)
counter_positions: Personhood is not the relevant moral category here

questions.question: Should the moral status of a being be decided by what it currently IS or by what it has the potential to BECOME?
positions: Potentiality grounds moral status (Marquis FLO)
Only actual capacities count (Singer)
counter_positions: Both matter but for different reasons

questions.question: If an embryo could be a future Mozart, does that change the ethics of destroying it?
positions: Yes — losing a future of value is the wrong (Marquis)
No — what matters is current sentience (Singer)
counter_positions: Counterfactual reasoning misleads moral judgement

questions.question: Are we radically free to choose who we become, or are we shaped by forces beyond our control?
positions: Sartre: existence precedes essence — we are radically free
Determinist: choices are products of biology and history
counter_positions: Compatibilism: freedom is compatible with causal embedding

questions.question: What is bad faith, and why does Sartre think it is the central ethical failure?
positions: Bad faith is denying our own freedom (Sartre)
Bad faith is just a useful coping mechanism (psychological response)
counter_positions: The concept of bad faith presupposes contestable claims about freedom

questions.question: If life has no inherent meaning, does that liberate us or does it produce despair?
positions: Camus: the absurd liberates (Sisyphus is happy)
Nihilist: despair is the honest response
counter_positions: Religious response: meaning is given, not made

questions.question: Is it ethical to live by social roles (student, daughter, employee), or is that a form of bad faith?
positions: Roles are inauthentic (Sartre on the waiter)
Roles can be authentically chosen and inhabited
counter_positions: The bad-faith critique presupposes a false individualism

questions.question: Does Beauvoir's analysis of women's situation show that 'authenticity' is harder for some people than others?
positions: Yes — situation conditions freedom (Beauvoir)
Sartre's freedom is universal regardless of situation
counter_positions: The existentialist framework fails for structurally oppressed people

questions.question: Can we be authentic in a culture saturated with social media performance?
positions: Yes — the structure of choice still applies (Sartrean reading)
No — performance has colonised the self (cultural critique)
counter_positions: The dichotomy of authentic vs performed is itself confused

questions.question: If we make ourselves through our choices, do we owe nothing to who we were before?
positions: Past selves don't bind future selves (radical Sartrean)
Past commitments create obligations even after we change
counter_positions: The question presupposes a discontinuous self

questions.question: Is suicide a meaningful response to absurdity, or its philosophical refusal?
positions: Camus: suicide refuses the absurd by escaping it; revolt is the answer
Suicide can be a coherent existentialist choice
counter_positions: The question misframes psychological reality as philosophy

questions.question: What is the role of attention in moral life, according to Iris Murdoch?
positions: Attention IS the moral act (Murdoch)
Action, not attention, is what counts morally (consequentialist)
counter_positions: Attention is a precondition but not itself moral

questions.question: Can we be good without trying to be — or does virtue require deliberate cultivation?
positions: Virtue requires habituation (Aristotle)
Murdoch: goodness emerges from loving attention, not effort of will
counter_positions: Virtue is partly innate, partly circumstantial

questions.question: Is loving attention to particular people a more reliable moral guide than abstract principles?
positions: Yes — attention reveals what principles miss (Murdoch)
No — attention without principle is bias
counter_positions: Both are needed; neither alone is enough

questions.question: Does the Murdochian mother-in-law example show that moral change is possible without action?
positions: Yes — inner re-seeing is moral progress (Murdoch)
No — moral progress requires behavioural change
counter_positions: The example begs the question of what counts as 'moral'

questions.question: Is character more important than action in deciding what is morally good?
positions: Virtue ethics (Aristotle, Murdoch): character is primary
Consequentialism: actions and outcomes are primary
Deontology: duty is primary
counter_positions: Different domains call for different priorities

questions.question: Can a Year 11 student today live a 'good life' in Aristotle's or Murdoch's sense?
positions: Yes — the requirements are universal
No — Aristotle's polis-based ethics doesn't translate
Partially — the project translates but the form changes
counter_positions: The 'good life' framing is itself culturally specific

questions.question: Are emotions reliable guides to moral truth, or do they distort our judgement?
positions: Nussbaum: emotions are a form of moral cognition
Stoic/Kantian: emotions cloud rational judgement
counter_positions: Some emotions are reliable, others not — depends on cultivation

questions.question: What does Nussbaum mean by 'narrative imagination,' and is it morally important?
positions: Narrative imagination is essential to compassion (Nussbaum)
Moral judgement should be principle-based, not narrative
counter_positions: Narrative imagination is helpful but not necessary

questions.question: Should moral education prioritise feeling (compassion) or reasoning (logic)?
positions: Feeling — compassion grounds moral motivation (Nussbaum)
Reasoning — emotions are unreliable without cognitive correction
counter_positions: Both, integrated: emotion as cognition (Nussbaum's actual position)

questions.question: Is compassion for distant strangers really possible, or is it a moral pretense?
positions: Yes — narrative imagination extends compassion (Nussbaum)
No — compassion at scale is psychologically incoherent (critic)
counter_positions: Distant compassion is real but operates differently

questions.question: If emotions are forms of judgement, can we be wrong to feel them?
positions: Yes — emotions can be irrational (Nussbaum)
No — emotions are responses, not judgements
counter_positions: The question conflates types of emotion

questions.question: Does literature make us better people — or is that a comforting myth literary critics tell themselves?
positions: Yes — narrative imagination is moral training (Nussbaum)
No — empirical evidence is weak
counter_positions: Literature affects character but not predictably

questions.question: Are our moral judgements really driven by intuition, with reasoning coming after the fact?
positions: Yes — Haidt's social intuitionist model
No — reasoning genuinely produces moral conclusions (rationalist)
counter_positions: Reasoning and intuition interact in complex ways

questions.question: Are there moral foundations (like loyalty, sanctity) that liberals and conservatives weigh differently?
positions: Yes — Moral Foundations Theory (Haidt)
No — these are biases dressed up as moral principles
counter_positions: The empirical evidence for MFT is contested

questions.question: Should we trust gut moral feelings, or treat them as biases to be overruled?
positions: Trust gut feelings — they encode evolved wisdom (Haidt-friendly)
Overrule them — careful reasoning is more reliable
counter_positions: Selectively trust — depends on which gut feeling

questions.question: If conservatives and progressives use different moral foundations, can either side be 'wrong'?
positions: No — different foundations are equally valid (cultural pluralism)
Yes — some foundations track moral truth better than others
counter_positions: The question presupposes that political views map onto moral foundations

questions.question: Can a Haidt-style descriptive account of morality ever yield normative conclusions?
positions: Yes — knowing how morality works informs how it should work
No — Hume's is/ought gap forbids this move
counter_positions: Sophisticated naturalists can bridge the gap

questions.question: Is religion necessary for morality, or do we have good moral reasons independent of God?
positions: Religion grounds morality (divine command theory)
Morality stands independent of religion (Plato's Euthyphro problem)
counter_positions: Religion strengthens but doesn't ground morality (Haidt)

questions.question: Does Haidt's account of religion's binding role show that secular societies lose something morally?
positions: Yes — religion binds groups in morally productive ways (Haidt)
No — secular institutions do the same work without supernatural baggage
counter_positions: Religion does both moral binding AND moral harm

questions.question: Is the Euthyphro dilemma fatal to divine command theory?
positions: Yes — either God commands the good, or the good is what God commands (vicious choice)
No — modern theological responses dissolve the dilemma
counter_positions: The dilemma applies only to crude versions of the theory

questions.question: Can a person without religious belief have a fully meaningful moral life?
positions: Yes — secular ethics is sufficient (humanist)
Possible but harder — religion provides scaffolding most people need (Haidt-adjacent)
counter_positions: The question wrongly treats meaning and morality as separable

questions.question: Should religious moral views have a place in public political debate?
positions: Yes — they are reasoned views like any other
No — public reason should be religiously neutral (Rawlsian)
counter_positions: Some religious views can enter, others can't — turns on translatability

questions.question: Is religious ethics fundamentally about loyalty/sanctity/authority, while secular ethics is about care/fairness?
positions: Yes — Haidt's empirical claim
No — both ethics span all foundations
counter_positions: The dichotomy is too neat

questions.question: Is genetic enhancement of children morally permissible, or does it wrong them?
positions: Permissible — parents already shape children profoundly
Wrong — it instrumentalises the future person
counter_positions: Permissible only for some traits (treatment) but not others (enhancement)

questions.question: Does the fictional society of Brave New World show that engineered happiness is not real happiness?
positions: Yes — depth and meaning require struggle
No — happiness is happiness regardless of cause
counter_positions: The novel begs the question of what 'real' happiness is

questions.question: If we could eliminate suffering entirely, should we?
positions: Yes — suffering has no intrinsic value (utilitarian)
No — some suffering is morally and existentially important
counter_positions: The choice would unmake what makes us human

questions.question: Is there a meaningful moral difference between 'treatment' and 'enhancement'?
positions: Yes — treatment restores normal function; enhancement exceeds it
No — the line is arbitrary and shifts with culture
counter_positions: The distinction matters for some purposes but not others

questions.question: Does the genetic supermarket reduce children to consumer products?
positions: Yes — choosing traits commodifies the child (Sandel-style)
No — parents already make consequential choices for children
counter_positions: The framing applies in some social arrangements but not all

questions.question: Do non-human animals have rights, or only welfare-claims?
positions: Rights — extended from human rights frameworks (Regan)
Welfare — utilitarian calculus (Singer)
counter_positions: Neither — animals are owed moral consideration of a different kind

questions.question: Is eating meat morally indefensible, given what we know about animal sentience?
positions: Yes — equal consideration of interests (Singer)
No — there are morally relevant differences between humans and animals
counter_positions: Some farming practices are defensible; others not

questions.question: If an alien species were demonstrably more intelligent than us, would they be entitled to treat us the way we treat factory-farmed animals?
positions: Yes — by the logic of speciesism (Singer-style reductio)
No — the analogy fails because moral status isn't a function of intelligence
counter_positions: The thought experiment illuminates the moral question without settling it

questions.question: Can we ever be certain of anything, or is all knowledge provisional?
positions: Some knowledge is certain (foundationalist)
All knowledge is fallible (fallibilist)
counter_positions: Different domains require different standards

questions.question: Is 'I think therefore I am' really immune to doubt?
positions: Yes — the cogito is self-validating (Descartes)
No — even the 'I' is a doubtful inference
counter_positions: The cogito proves something but not what Descartes thought

questions.question: Can knowledge come from intuition, or only from reasoning and evidence?
positions: Intuition is a legitimate source (rationalist)
Only reasoning and evidence count (empiricist)
counter_positions: Intuitions ARE evidence — they require interpretation

questions.question: If two thoughtful people examine the same evidence and reach opposite conclusions, what does that tell us about knowledge?
positions: Knowledge is genuinely difficult; disagreement is to be expected
Disagreement reveals that at least one party has biases or missing data
Disagreement shows knowledge is partly subjective
counter_positions: Different cognitive styles produce different but legitimate conclusions

questions.question: Is doubt itself a kind of knowledge?
positions: Yes — knowing what we don't know is real cognitive achievement (Socratic)
No — doubt is the absence of knowledge
counter_positions: The distinction depends on what 'knowledge' means

questions.question: Can we trust 'common sense' as a guide to truth?
positions: Yes — it embodies inherited wisdom
No — common sense has been wrong about almost everything important
counter_positions: Common sense is reliable in some domains, unreliable in others

questions.question: Can we trust our senses to deliver accurate information about the world?
positions: Yes — perception evolved to track reality (broadly reliabilist)
No — perception systematically distorts (sceptic)
counter_positions: Perception is reliable for some purposes but not others

questions.question: If our senses can be deceived, how can we know we're not always being deceived?
positions: We can't — global scepticism is unanswerable (Cartesian)
We can — pragmatic and inferential responses suffice
counter_positions: The question is incoherent — global doubt undermines itself

questions.question: Are colours real properties of objects, or constructions of our minds?
positions: Real properties (naïve realism)
Mental constructions (subjectivist)
Relational properties (dispositional view)
counter_positions: The answer depends on what 'real' means

questions.question: Is there a real world independent of our perception of it?
positions: Yes — realism
No — idealism (Berkeley)
The question is unanswerable (agnostic)
counter_positions: The dichotomy is misframed

questions.question: Could two people perceive the same scene and have genuinely different visual experiences?
positions: Yes — perception is theory-laden
No — basic perception is shared, interpretation differs
counter_positions: The question of 'sameness' of experience is itself unclear

questions.question: Is what we call 'reality' just the consensus of well-functioning perceivers?
positions: Yes — reality is intersubjective construction (constructivist)
No — reality is what would exist absent any perceivers (realist)
counter_positions: Both positions miss the role of language and concept

questions.question: Can emotions give us knowledge that reasoning cannot?
positions: Yes — emotions track features of situations (Nussbaum)
No — emotions are unreliable noise on top of cognition
counter_positions: Emotions are cognitive evaluations — the dichotomy is false

questions.question: Is fear a form of knowing — or just a physiological response?
positions: A form of knowing — fear discriminates real from imagined danger (Nussbaum)
Pure response — fear is sub-cognitive
counter_positions: Fear is both — a cognitive evaluation embodied in physiology

questions.question: Can compassion produce genuine knowledge about others' inner lives?
positions: Yes — narrative imagination accesses what abstraction cannot (Nussbaum)
No — compassion can mislead about others' actual states
counter_positions: Compassion is one input to knowledge, not knowledge itself

questions.question: Should we trust our gut feelings about people more than our careful judgements?
positions: Yes — gut feelings are pattern-recognition (Haidt-friendly)
No — careful judgement corrects for bias
counter_positions: Each is reliable in different domains

questions.question: When is it rational to trust an expert, and when should we think for ourselves?
positions: Defer to experts in their domain
Always think for yourself; experts can be wrong
counter_positions: Trust experts but verify; reserve final judgement

questions.question: Is testimony a genuine source of knowledge, or only an inferior substitute?
positions: Genuine source — most of what we know comes from others
Inferior — first-hand knowledge is the gold standard
counter_positions: Testimony is genuine but its reliability depends on context

questions.question: If we trust an expert who turns out to be wrong, were we irrational to trust them?
positions: No — rational trust can lead to false belief
Yes — we should have reserved judgement
counter_positions: Depends on whether warning signs were available

questions.question: Should we trust testimony from people whose moral foundations differ from ours?
positions: Yes — they may see what we miss (Haidt's lesson)
No — their interpretation will systematically differ from ours
counter_positions: Trust on factual matters but not evaluative ones

questions.question: Does it matter what the artist meant when we interpret a work of art?
positions: No — the intentional fallacy makes intention irrelevant (Wimsatt & Beardsley)
Yes — meaning is what the artist intended
counter_positions: Intention is one consideration among many

questions.question: Can a poem mean something the poet didn't intend?
positions: Yes — meaning is in the text, not the mind (Wimsatt & Beardsley)
No — meaning requires an intender
counter_positions: Some meanings are intended, others emerge

questions.question: If we can never directly access the artist's intention, does the intentional fallacy actually matter?
positions: Yes — appeal to intention is fallacious AND practically pointless
No — we infer intention from the work, which is fine
counter_positions: The argument is sound for some genres, weak for others

questions.question: Does reader-response criticism ('what it means to me') give us knowledge of the work, or only of ourselves?
positions: Of the work — meaning IS the reader's response
Of ourselves — only — the work has its own meaning
counter_positions: Some readings illuminate the work; others only the reader

questions.question: Should an artist's biography (or politics, or crimes) affect how we interpret their work?
positions: No — Wimsatt & Beardsley's intentional fallacy
Yes — context shapes meaning
counter_positions: Affects interpretation of some works, not others

questions.question: If a viral video accidentally captures something profound, does it become art?
positions: Yes — meaning emerges from reception, not making (Wimsatt & Beardsley extension)
No — art requires intention
counter_positions: 'Art' as a category is contested; the question begs definition

questions.question: Is science the only path to genuine knowledge?
positions: Yes — scientism
No — humanities and arts produce knowledge of a different kind
counter_positions: Different domains, different methods, all genuine

questions.question: Can science answer ethical questions, or only describe what is?
positions: Only describe — Hume's is/ought gap
Can answer some — empirical findings constrain ethics
counter_positions: Science is a partner to ethics, neither master nor servant

questions.question: If science keeps revising itself, can it ever give us truth — or only the latest provisional model?
positions: Provisional only (Popper-style fallibilism)
Convergent on truth (scientific realism)
counter_positions: Science is reliable methodology even without final truth

questions.question: Is consciousness something science can in principle explain?
positions: Yes — neuroscience will eventually solve it (functionalist)
No — the hard problem of consciousness is fundamentally philosophical
counter_positions: Science can explain neural correlates but not subjective experience

questions.question: Is the mind nothing more than the brain?
positions: Yes — identity theory (mind = brain states)
No — dualism (mind and brain are distinct)
Yes but not in the way identity theory says — functionalism
counter_positions: The mind/brain question may be incoherent as posed

questions.question: Could a perfect computer simulation of a brain be conscious?
positions: Yes — functionalism (multiple realisability)
No — biological naturalism
Maybe — depends on whether consciousness is substrate-dependent
counter_positions: The question may be empirically unanswerable

questions.question: What makes a mental state 'about' something — what fixes meaning?
positions: Functional role determines meaning (functionalism)
Causal connection to the world (externalism)
Internal qualitative character (internalism)
counter_positions: Meaning is multiply determined

questions.question: Is dualism viable in the 21st century, or has neuroscience killed it?
positions: Killed it — neural correlates leave no room for non-physical mind
Still viable — neural correlates are not identity
counter_positions: Property dualism survives even if substance dualism doesn't

questions.question: Is there 'something it is like' to be a bat — and what does the question reveal about consciousness?
positions: Yes — Nagel's argument shows consciousness has irreducibly subjective character
No — bat experience is reducible to physical descriptions
counter_positions: Nagel shows our concept of objectivity is incomplete

questions.question: Could there be a being that behaves exactly like a conscious person but has no inner experience (a 'philosophical zombie')?
positions: Yes conceivable, therefore consciousness is non-physical (Chalmers)
Inconceivable — behaviour and consciousness can't come apart
counter_positions: Conceivability doesn't prove possibility

questions.question: If a lobotomy changes someone's personality, is the new person still 'them'?
positions: Yes — same body, same brain, same person
No — radical mental change is a kind of death
counter_positions: Identity comes in degrees here

questions.question: Are emotions mental states with intentional content, or just bodily feelings?
positions: Intentional states with content (Nussbaum)
Bodily feelings only (James-Lange)
counter_positions: Both — emotions involve cognitive content AND bodily response

questions.question: Are you the same person you were when you were five years old?
positions: Yes — bodily continuity (animalism)
Yes — psychological continuity (Locke)
No — you are a series of related but distinct selves
counter_positions: The question of strict identity may be unanswerable

questions.question: If your memories were perfectly transferred to a new body, would 'you' have moved?
positions: Yes — psychological continuity (Locke)
No — bodily continuity is essential (animalism)
counter_positions: The case is too underspecified to answer

questions.question: If you were duplicated perfectly, which one would be 'you'?
positions: Both equally
Neither — duplication shows identity isn't preserved
The original only
counter_positions: The question reveals our concept of identity is confused

questions.question: Does personal identity matter morally, or is it just a useful fiction?
positions: Matters — responsibility, promises, and obligations require identity
Useful fiction — what matters is psychological connectedness (Parfit)
counter_positions: Identity matters less than we think but more than nothing

questions.question: Should we hold the 60-year-old to promises made by the 20-year-old?
positions: Yes — same person, same obligations
No — psychological discontinuity weakens the obligation
counter_positions: Depends on the type of promise

questions.question: Is death the end of you, or just the end of this version of you?
positions: The end — no afterlife, no continuation (materialist)
Just this version — continuation in some form
counter_positions: The question presupposes a problematic conception of self

questions.question: Could we be living in a computer simulation, and how would we know?
positions: Possibly — simulation argument (Bostrom-style)
No — the hypothesis is unfalsifiable and so empty
counter_positions: The question is intelligible but not empirically tractable

questions.question: If we are in a simulation, would anything we care about become less real or less valuable?
positions: Yes — relationships and achievements would be downgraded
No — what matters is the experience, not the substrate
counter_positions: Some things would be devalued, others not

questions.question: Is the simulation hypothesis a modern version of Descartes' evil demon?
positions: Yes — same structure of global doubt
No — simulation is empirically grounded; demons are not
counter_positions: Structurally similar but motivationally different

questions.question: If reality is virtual, is virtue still real?
positions: Yes — virtue is about character, not substrate
No — virtue requires a stake in real outcomes
counter_positions: Depends on what 'real' means

questions.question: Would discovering we're in a simulation be liberating or devastating?
positions: Liberating — frees us from cosmic insignificance
Devastating — undermines all our projects
counter_positions: Most people would adapt and continue much as before

questions.question: If our minds could be uploaded, would the upload be us?
positions: Yes — psychological continuity preserved (Locke + functionalism)
No — the upload is at best a copy
counter_positions: The upload is something new — neither the original nor a mere copy

questions.question: Are we genuinely free, or is every choice the product of prior causes?
positions: Free — libertarianism / Sartrean radical freedom
Determined — hard determinism
Both — compatibilism
counter_positions: The question may presuppose an incoherent concept of freedom

questions.question: If determinism is true, can anyone be morally responsible for anything?
positions: No — responsibility requires alternative possibilities (libertarian)
Yes — responsibility requires only the right kind of causation (compatibilist)
counter_positions: Responsibility is a social practice, not a metaphysical fact

questions.question: Is Sartre's 'we are condemned to be free' a description or a slogan?
positions: Description — accurate phenomenology of choice
Slogan — overstates the actual range of freedom
counter_positions: Both — descriptive of phenomenology, but exaggerated

questions.question: Does neuroscience showing pre-conscious decision-making mean free will is illusory?
positions: Yes — Libet-style findings refute folk free will
No — pre-conscious processing is part of, not opposed to, freedom
counter_positions: The neuroscience is over-interpreted

questions.question: If we never could have done otherwise, is regret a coherent attitude?
positions: No — regret presupposes alternative possibilities
Yes — regret tracks evaluative comparison, not metaphysical possibility
counter_positions: Regret is an evolved emotion, not a philosophical claim

questions.question: Are addictions a genuine threat to free will, or a misuse of the concept?
positions: Genuine threat — addiction overrides agency
Misuse — addicts choose, just under harder conditions
counter_positions: Both — addiction reveals freedom is gradient, not binary

questions.question: Does Sartre's 'bad faith' presuppose that we have more freedom than we usually admit, or less?
positions: More — bad faith is denial of our actual freedom
Less — Sartre overestimates the freedom we have
counter_positions: The concept is internally unstable

questions.question: Is structural oppression compatible with Sartrean radical freedom, or does it expose its limits?
positions: Compatible — Beauvoir's extension shows situation conditions but doesn't abolish freedom
Limits — Sartre's freedom presupposes privilege
counter_positions: The framework is salvageable with substantial revision

questions.question: Is death bad for the person who dies?
positions: Yes — deprivation account: death deprives one of future goods
No — Epicurus: where death is, I am not
counter_positions: Bad in some senses (deprivation), not in others (no suffering)

questions.question: Would immortality be a blessing or a curse?
positions: Curse — eternal life would lose all stakes (Williams)
Blessing — more time for what matters
Depends on the kind of immortality
counter_positions: The question wrongly treats immortality as univocal

questions.question: Does mortality give life its meaning, or merely its terror?
positions: Mortality gives meaning — finite stakes (Heidegger-adjacent)
Mortality is just terror; meaning would survive its abolition
counter_positions: Mortality is one source of meaning among many

questions.question: Is fear of death rational, or a relic of evolution we should grow out of?
positions: Rational — death is the loss of all goods
Irrelic — Epicurean: nothing to fear once you understand
counter_positions: Some fear is rational (process), some not (state)

questions.question: If your atoms were perfectly reassembled after death, would you be back?
positions: Yes — material identity reconstituted
No — continuity, not material composition, makes you you
counter_positions: The thought experiment reveals our concept is confused

questions.question: Does Camus's claim that 'we must imagine Sisyphus happy' make sense in the face of real suffering?
positions: Yes — revolt is the right response to absurdity
No — Camus romanticises suffering
counter_positions: The myth works as metaphor, not literal recommendation

questions.question: What is a work of art — the physical object, the artist's intention, or the viewer's experience?
positions: The physical object
The artist's intention
The viewer's experience
The intersection of all three
counter_positions: 'Work of art' is not a single thing but a family of overlapping uses

questions.question: Is a poem the marks on the page, the sounds when read, or the meaning?
positions: The marks (formal)
The sounds (performance)
The meaning (semantic)
counter_positions: A poem is all three — each is partial

questions.question: Does a piece of art exist when no-one is experiencing it?
positions: Yes — works persist independent of perception
No — works exist only in their experiences
counter_positions: Different ontologies for different art-types

questions.question: Is a forgery genuinely worth less than the original, even when indistinguishable?
positions: Yes — historical authenticity matters
No — aesthetic value is in what we perceive
counter_positions: Worth depends on the kind of value at issue

questions.question: If meaning is in the work, not the artist, can a found object be art?
positions: Yes — Duchamp's urinal proves it
No — art requires intentional making
counter_positions: The institutional theory of art settles this differently

questions.question: Is AI-generated art genuinely art, given there is no intending mind behind it?
positions: Yes — Wimsatt & Beardsley's intentional fallacy applies
No — art requires intention
Yes but the prompter is the artist
counter_positions: The category of 'art' may need rethinking, not extension
